coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot/nickel nanocomposite coating for wear and corrosion control of Mg alloy: Experimental and theoretical studies
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Shengguo
Liu, Zhijing
Lu, Zhibin
Ma, Liqiu
coredata.dc:description: Due to their self-lubrication and corrosion inhibition properties, carbon dots (CDs) were utilized to endow a nickel coating on a magnesium alloy substrate with superior tribology and anti-corrosion properties. The surface and cross-sectional morphologies confirm that these novel carbon dots remarkably improve the compactness of the nickel coating, leading to much higher hardness and adhesion strength. The carbon dot/nickel coating prepared with 0.5 g/L CDs has a lower wear rate and friction coefficient than the other coatings, demonstrating its excellent self-lubricating performance. Moreover, as demonstrated by electrochemical tests and the formation of corrosion products, this coating exhibits significantly enhanced anti-corrosion performance, with the lowest corrosion current and largest impedance modulus among the prepared coatings. Theoretical calculations confirm that the CDs provide a high energy barrier for the penetrating diffusion of Cl. Thus, the CDs effectively prevent the active penetration of corrosive substances. As a result, the as-prepared carbon dot/nickel nanocomposite coatings can efficiently control the wear and corrosion failures of Mg alloy under some harsh conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nickel coating
Wear
Corrosion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of electroplated carbon dot/nickel coatings
Characterization of carbon dot/nickel composite coatings

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by funding from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52065025), the Jiangxi Provincial Department of Education (Grant No. GJJ2200827), and the Youth Jinggang Scholars Program in Jiangxi Province.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram of preparation process for obtaining CDs and nickel coating.
SEM surface images of carbon dot/nickel coatings prepared in different CD concentrations: (a) 0.1 g/L; (b) 0.3 g/L; (c) 0.5 g/L; (d) 0.8 g/L; (e) 1 g/L.
AFM images of carbon dot/nickel coatings prepared in different CD concentrations: (a) 0.1 g/L; (b) 0.3 g/L; (c) 0.5 g/L; (d) 0.8 g/L; (e) 1 g/L.

SEM cross-sectional morphologies, EDS spectra, and (inset) elemental mapping of carbon dot/nickel coatings prepared in different CD concentrations: (a) 0.1 g/L; (b) 0.3 g/L; (c) 0.5 g/L; (d) 0.8 g/L; (e) 1 g/L.
XRD patterns of carbon dot/nickel coatings prepared in different CD concentrations.
Water contact angles of the carbon dot/nickel coatings prepared in different CD concentrations.
Microhardness and critical loading values of carbon dot/nickel coatings prepared in different CD concentrations.
Scratch track micrographs of carbon dot/nickel coatings prepared in different CD concentrations.
Friction coefficient and specific wear rate values of carbon dot/nickel coatings prepared in different CD concentrations.
3D profiles and SEM micrographs of carbon dot/nickel coating wear tracks: (a) 0.1 g/L; (b) 0.3 g/L; (c) 0.5 g/L; (d) 0.8 g/L; (e) 1 g/L.
Electrochemical analysis of carbon dot/nickel coatings: (a) polarization curves; (b) impedance spectra; (c) impedance modulus; (d) phase angle; (e) Rf and equivalent circuit diagram.
SEM morphologies and EDS mapping of corrosion products of the carbon dot/nickel coatings prepared in different CD concentrations: (a) 0.1 g/L; (b) 0.3 g/L; (c) 0.5 g/L; (d) 0.8 g/L; (e) 1 g/L.
Differential charge densities of Cl adsorption on (a) nickel surface and (c) carbon dot; (b,d) the corresponding PDOS.
Energy barriers and diffusion paths of Cl penetration through (a,b) single-layered nickel surface and (c,d) carbon dot.
Schematic diagram for anti-corrosion mechanism of the carbon dot/nickel coating.
Pulse electroplating process and electrochemical solution composition.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot/nickel nanocomposite coating for wear and corrosion control of Mg alloy: Experimental and theoretical studies

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou 341000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou 341000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou 341000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou 341000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to their self-lubrication and corrosion inhibition properties, carbon dots (CDs) were utilized to endow a nickel coating on a magnesium alloy substrate with superior tribology and anti-corrosion properties. The surface and cross-sectional morphologies confirm that these novel carbon dots remarkably improve the compactness of the nickel coating, leading to much higher hardness and adhesion strength. The carbon dot/nickel coating prepared with 0.5 g/L CDs has a lower wear rate and friction coefficient than the other coatings, demonstrating its excellent self-lubricating performance. Moreover, as demonstrated by electrochemical tests and the formation of corrosion products, this coating exhibits significantly enhanced anti-corrosion performance, with the lowest corrosion current and largest impedance modulus among the prepared coatings. Theoretical calculations confirm that the CDs provide a high energy barrier for the penetrating diffusion of Cl. Thus, the CDs effectively prevent the active penetration of corrosive substances. As a result, the as-prepared carbon dot/nickel nanocomposite coatings can efficiently control the wear and corrosion failures of Mg alloy under some harsh conditions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Magnesium alloy materials are commonly employed in vehicles, electronics, precision instruments, and the aerospace and other industries because they have high strength-to-weight ratios, low densities, high impact load resistances, large elastic modulus values, good thermal conductivity, and excellent casting properties[1–4]. Unfortunately, magnesium alloys are vulnerable to corrosion and exhibit wear disadvantages, which limit their application. Therefore, improving the surface characteristics of magnesium alloys is a significant research focus. Techniques for improving surface properties include chemical immersion plating, micro-arc oxidation, chemical or physical vapor deposition, and pulsed electrodeposition[5–9]. Among these strategies, pulse electrodeposition is a simple and flexible process. Pulse electrodeposition methods utilize the relaxation of the diffusion layer during on–off cycling to restore the concentration of metal ions at the cathode, which is conducive to the deposition of metal ions and formation of a high-quality coating[10] . Pulse electroplated nickel-based coatings show particularly good promise as a protective strategy due to their strong adhesion strength, non-toxicity, and tendency to passivate in various media such as water and saline solutions[11,12]. However, electroplated nickel coatings with single structures cannot provide sufficient protection in specific environments, often leading to severe corrosion and friction failures in some harsh working conditions. The characteristics of as-prepared nickel coatings can be improved using additive materials such as strengthening particles. These additives include SiO2, SiC, and graphene[13–15]. Thus, developing novel nanoscale strengthening particles to provide nickel-based coatings with superior anti-corrosion and tribological performance would be highly significant for the protection of Mg alloys.

Carbon dots (CDs) are highly promising novel carbon-based nanomaterials with diameters of less than 10 nm that have gained significant attention due to their unique properties[16–18]. The excellent physicochemical properties and layered structure of CDs provide them with broad application potential in the field of lubricant and inhibitor additives[19]. The addition of CDs as an inhibitor and lubricator can effectively prevent corrosion failure and friction failure of coatings. This is because the surface of CDs contains an abundance of nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups that can adsorb onto positively charged sites on the friction surface, where they act as “bearing balls” that react with worn surfaces to form a carbon-based lubricating film[20–22]. Moreover, while hydrochloric acid solutions are highly corrosive environments for steel, the application of N-doped CDs can prevent this corrosion[23]. The Cl− ion in hydrochloric acid can induce intrinsic adsorption, helping the steel surface adsorb the inhibitor[24]. Therefore, CDs are often used to modify composite coatings to improve their protective ability. Examples of such coatings include CD-modified epoxy resin, CD-modified urea formaldehyde resin, and N-CD/PDA composite coatings[25–27]. However, only a few studies have investigated the incorporation of CDs into nickel-based coatings, and the mechanisms by which CDs contribute to the corrosion and friction behavior of coatings based on nickel have not been sufficiently explored. Consequently, developing insight into the characteristics of CDs in nickel-based coatings would be of extreme significance for improving the properties of magnesium-based alloys.

Herein, novel CDs were employed as an effective self-lubricator and corrosion inhibitor to prepare a series of robust carbon dot/nickel nanocomposite coatings on Mg alloy substrates by pulse electroplating technique. The microstructural and mechanical characteristics of coatings prepared with different CD content were investigated in detail. Then, the effects of CDs on the anti-wear and anti-corrosion performance of the as-prepared nickel-based coatings were discussed. A first-principles theoretical study was performed to further explore the role of CDs in enhancing the coating performance. Finally, the failure mechanism of the carbon dot/nickel nanocomposite coatings was proposed and discussed. This work offers a unique strategy and guidance for enhancing the tribology and anti-corrosion performance of nickel-based coatings, which show great promise for Mg alloy protection.

AZ91D magnesium alloy (Dongguan Metal Co. Ltd., China) was cut into substrates with dimensions of 55 × 18 × 5 mm. The surface of each magnesium alloy substrate was polished using 400–1500 grit silicon carbide (SiC) sandpaper until no scratches or dullness remained. Subsequently, the polished magnesium alloys were cleaned via ultrasonication in an acetone bath for 30 min.

All chemicals used in this work were analytical-grade. Before performing electrochemical deposition, each Mg alloy substrate surface was pretreated to enhance the bonding and adhesion of the substrate to the as-prepared coatings. The pretreatment process and main parameters are listed in Table 1 . Smooth surface pretreatment was ensured by carrying out each step using constant-temperature water bath heating. Magnetic stirring was employed to maintain equilibrium ion concentrations and minimize experimental interference. After each pretreatment step, the Mg alloy substrates were rinsed using deionized water to minimize the effects of residual solutions from the previous steps on subsequent operations.

The carbon dots (CDs) were hydrothermally synthesized. First, 2 g ammonium citrate was added to distilled water to form an ammonium citrate solution. This ammonium citrate solution was hydrothermally reacted for 6 h at 200 °C to acquire a solution containing particles. Next, this solution was dialyzed, followed by drying until CDs powders were obtained, and the synthesized carbon dots (CDs) were N-doped CDs. The process for obtaining these CDs is shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of electroplated carbon dot/nickel coatings
ce:section.ce:para: Nickel electroplating solutions with varying CD content (0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.8, 1 g/L CDs) were prepared. Each electrochemical solution underwent ultrasonication for 30 min in a water bath to effectively and uniformly disperse the CDs. Finally, pulse electroplating was employed to obtain carbon dot/nickel nanocomposite coatings with varying CD content. The electroplating process and relevant solutions are shown in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon dot/nickel composite coatings
ce:section.ce:para: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Carl Zeiss Evo18, Japan) was operated at 25 kV for morphological (surface and cross-section) evaluation. SEM was paired with energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) to determine elemental compositions. Morphologies were characterized in more detail by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL20OCX, Japan). Crystal structures were evaluated with X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany) using Kα radiation in the 2θ range of 10-90° (50 kV voltage, 100 mA current, 0.04° step size). Surface wettability was characterized using a contact angle meter (JY-82A, China). Hardness was studied with a Vickers hardness tester (HVT-1000A, China) using a 500 gf load. A CSM scratch tester (CSM Instruments Revetest) was employed to study coating-substrate adhesion. The scratch length was 3 mm, the load range was 0–100 N, and the scratch duration was 1.5 mm/min. An atomic force microscope was used to measure surface roughness (AFM, Yuan Nano-Scanning Instrument, BY2000, China). Friction and wear performance was evaluated with a reciprocating friction tester (MFT-R4000, China). The sliding length was 5 mm, the normal load was 5 N, the sliding time was 30 min, and the sliding frequency was 5 Hz. The three-dimensional contour of the wear marks was observed using a 3D optical profilometer (model: UP-Lambda America). Corrosion behavior was studied with a three-electrode system (1 cm2 coated sample, Pt plate, and saturated potassium chloride (KCl) as the working, counter, and reference electrodes) using a CHI660E workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai, China).

The Vienna ab initio Software Package (VASP) was used with the Perdew Burke Emzerhof (PBE) generalized gradient approximation (GGA) to perform density functional theory (DFT) calculations [28,29]. Structural optimization was performed without any symmetric constraints by conjugate gradient method until the force was below 0.01 eV/Å and the exerted energy was below 1.0 × 10−5 eV/atom. The plane wave cut-off was 550 eV. Adjacent layers were separated with a perpendicular 15 Å vacuum gap to avoid artificial interactions between interlayers. The climbing image nudged elastic band (Cl-NEB) tool of VASP (VTST) developed by the Henkelman Group was employed to locate saddle points and determine minimal energy paths[30,31] .

TEM micrographs of the prepared CDs are exhibited in Fig. 2 . The as-prepared CDs show good dispersibility and are nearly spherical, indicating that they can be evenly dispersed in the electroplating solution. Cross-sectional EDS analysis of the electroplated Ni-based coatings shows the uniform dispersion of carbon (C) and nitrogen (N) elements, confirming the uniform dispersion of CDs in the electroplating solution.

SEM images were obtained to reveal the surface morphologies of the electroplated carbon dot/nickel nanocomposite coatings prepared with different CD doping concentrations, as shown in Fig. 3 . With increasing CD concentration, more CDs are incorporated on the coating surface. In particular, the carbon dot/nickel coating electroplated with a 0.5 g/L CD doping concentration exhibits a more uniform distribution of CDs and fewer surface defects than the other samples. However, at a higher CD doping concentration of 0.8 g/L, CD aggregation on the coating surface becomes more much severe. This could potentially lead to the easier delamination of the coating from the substrate.

AFM was used to study the three-dimensional morphologies of the carbon dot/nickel coatings, as shown in Fig. 4 . These images demonstrate that the surface roughness of the carbon dot/nickel coatings initially increases and then decreases as the CD content increases. The carbon dot/nickel coating electroplated using a 0.5 g/L CD doping concentration shows a high surface roughness (Ra = 12.1 nm) compared to most of the other coatings. However, the highest surface roughness is achieved with 0.8 g/L CDs, which is potentially attributed to the agglomeration of CDs favoring the formation of holes in the coating. The overall trend is basically consistent with the SEM analysis.

The cross-sections and EDS analysis of the carbon dot/nickel nanocomposite coatings are exhibited in Fig. 5 . These coatings have thicknesses of approximately 13.7 μm. All the magnesium alloy substrates show significant debris and particle features. This debris was mainly formed during the slicing process. Distinct boundaries and adhesive interfaces between the carbon dot/nickel nanocomposite coatings and magnesium alloy substrates can also be observed, indicating that these coatings effectively enhance the impact resistance of the magnesium alloy. The corresponding EDS spectra and maps indicate the existence of carbon (C) and nitrogen, confirming the even distribution of the CDs in the coatings. Some elemental diffusion can be observed at the interface, indicating the good metallurgical substrate-coating bonding.

Diffraction patterns of the carbon dot/nickel nanocomposite coatings are shown in Fig. 6 . The Ni (111), (200), and (220) peaks can be observed at 2θ = 44.77°, 52.01°, and 76.32°, respectively. No diffraction peaks attributed to the CDs and substrates can be detected. The absence of substrate peaks is attributed to the thick coatings completely covering the magnesium alloy substrates, and the relatively low CD content explains the lack of carbon peaks. The main growth direction of the carbon dot/nickel coating is primarily the Ni (111) crystal plane. The coatings have grain sizes of 9.22 nm (0.1 g/L CDs), 8.68 nm (0.3 g/L CDs), 8.0 nm (0.5 g/L CDs), 8.36 nm (0.8 g/L CDs), and 8.49 nm (1 g/L CDs), as determined by the Scherrer equation[32,33]. All calculated grain sizes are relatively fine, indicating that the CDs have a refining effect on the grain size. Thus, these nickel-based coatings have compacted structures. The smallest grain size is achieved by the carbon dot/nickel coating prepared using a CD concentration of 0.5 g/L, so this coating might present optimal frictional and corrosive performance.

Static water contact angles (WCAs) were obtained to demonstrate the wettability of the coatings[34,35]. With increasing CD concentration, the water contact angle first increases and decreases, as displayed in Fig. 7 . These coatings are hydrophilic (WCA < 90°). In general, surface wettability is influenced by surface energy and morphological features. The SEM surface images of the coatings confirm that the CDs provide a certain degree of roughness. The coating prepared with 0.5 g/L CDs has a more uniform CD distribution and superior surface roughness. Therefore, this coating also shows the highest water contact angle of 87.2°.

The microhardness values of the as-prepared carbon dot/nickel coatings are exhibited in Fig. 8 . The microhardness of the coating prepared with 0.5 g/L CDs is about 50 % higher than the hardness of a pure nickel coating (225 HV) and around 4 times higher than the hardness of the magnesium alloy (76 HV) [36]. Microhardness significantly increases as the CD doping concentration increases to 0.5 g/L. However, at CD concentrations above 0.5 g/L, the microhardness remains relatively stable in the range of 340 ± 3 HV. The refined grain sizes of these coatings (as determined by XRD analysis and Scherrer equation calculations) explain their improved mechanical performance. The small grain size of these coatings enhances their hardness. However, the coating prepared with a CD concentration has a grain size that is only slightly smaller compared to that achieved at 0.5 g/L, while the aggregation of the CDs becomes more severe. This enhanced aggregation might negatively affect the mechanical properties of this coating. Therefore, 0.5 g/L is the optimal CD doping concentration for achieving superior microhardness.

The scratch tracks and acoustic radiation curves of the as-prepared carbon dot/nickel coatings are shown in Fig. 9 . Scratch tests were performed to study coating-substrate adhesion. Generally, a high critical load value corresponds to good coating-substrate adhesion. With increasing CD concentration, the adhesion strength shows a trend of first increasing and then decreasing. This is potentially due to the CDs enhancing the stretchability of the as-prepared coatings, which would lead to reduced fracture. The coating synthesized with 0.5 g/L CDs shows the highest critical load of LC = 14.3 N, indicating its superior adhesion strength. The coatings prepared with higher CD doping concentrations are more aggregated, leading to deteriorated mechanical properties.

The friction coefficient (μ) and wear rate values of the as-prepared carbon dot/nickel nanocomposite coatings are shown in Fig. 10 . The lowest friction coefficient of μ = 0.39 is achieved by the coating synthesized with 0.5 g/L CDs. At higher or lower CD concentrations, μ increases. This is mainly due to the relatively superior microhardness and adhesion strength of the 0.5 g/L CDs coating. Moreover, the presence of carbon dots, which act as a self-lubricating agent, also effectively decreases the friction coefficient. However, when using 1 g/L CDs to prepare the coating, the friction coefficient rises to about 0.55. This is ascribed to the greater agglomeration of CDs under high doping concentrations, which negatively affects the coating adhesion during wear. The lowest wear rate (3.0 × 10−5 mm/N·m) is achieved by the coating synthesized with 0.5 g/L CDs. Thus, the wear rates show a similar trend. The better hardness of this coating is a key factor that explains its improved plastic deformation resistance and lower degree of wear. Moreover, the uniformly distributed CDs acting as a self-lubricating agent on this coating successfully improve its anti-wear performance. Consequently, this coating shows the best tribological properties.

3D profiles and SEM images of the carbon dot/nickel nanocomposite coatings are displayed in Fig. 11 . The width and depth of wear characteristics of these coatings initially decrease and then increase as the CD doping concentration increases. The 0.5 g/L CDs coating shows the smallest amount of wear, narrowest wear track width, and shallowest wear track depth. The other coatings prepared with lower or higher CD concentrations present a typical abrasive wear mechanism, as indicated by the presence of many obvious ploughing tracks. However, the carbon dot/nickel coating prepared in 0.5 g/L CDs only presents smooth wear. This analysis confirms that the optimal CD concentration of 0.5 g/L leads to superior tribological performance.

Potentiodynamic polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were carried out in 3.5 wt% sodium chloride solution to study the corrosion of the coatings. Coatings with good resistance to corrosion have low Icorr values or high Ecorr values[37,38]. As shown in Fig. 12 (a), the coatings show polarization potentials of −0.86 V (0.1 g/L), −0.78 V (0.3 g/L), −0.58 V (0.5 g/L), −0.69 V (0.8 g/L), and −0.87 V (1 g/L). The corrosion currents of these coatings are 9.086 × 10−5 A/cm2 (0.1 /gL), 2.947 × 10−6 A/cm2 (0.3 g/L), 7.1 × 10−7 A/cm2 (0.5 g/L), 2.648 × 10−6 A/cm2 (0.8 g/L), and 3.576 × 10−6 A/cm2 (1.0 g/L). The 0.5 g/L CDs coating has the highest Ecorr value and lowest Icorr value, demonstrating that embedding the optimum CD content in the nickel coating effectively inhibits the corrosive media. Lower corrosion resistance is achieved as the CD doping concentration increases above 0.5 g/L. This is explained by the aggregation of CDs negatively affecting the properties of the coatings. EIS was utilized to analyze coating corrosion behavior. In general, a larger capacitive loop in an EIS curve demonstrates better corrosion resistance[39–41]. With increasing CD doping concentration, the capacitive loop first increases and then decreases size, as shown in Fig. 12(b). The 0.5 g/L CDs coating exhibits the largest capacitive loop size, indicating the best corrosion resistance. These further demonstrate that there is an effective inhibition effect of CDs as an inhibitor or the influence of neutral anionic properties on the adsorption of inhibitors.

Fig. 12(c) displays the impedance modulus values of the carbon dot/nickel coatings. The coating prepared in 0.5 g/L CDs shows the largest impedance modulus. Fig. 12(d) shows the phase angles of the carbon dot/nickel coatings, demonstrating the same trend as the EIS and impedance modulus results. Zview software[42] was employed to investigate impedance changes, as displayed in Fig. 12(e). Rs is the resistance of the solution, Rct represents charge transfer resistance, Rf indicates the resistance of the film, and CPE represents the constant phase angle element[37]. The 0.5 g/L CDs coating shows the highest Rf value of 1.508 × 105 Ω·cm2. These results demonstrate that Cl− penetration is inhibited by the CDs, and 0.5 g/L CDs provides the optimal CD content for achieving excellent corrosion resistance.

The corrosion products were analyzed by EDS, as shown in Fig. 13 . Many loose corrosion products and pitting corrosion are observed on the surfaces of the carbon dot/nickel coatings prepared with lower CD content. However, with increasing CD doping concentration, the corrosion products and pitting corrosion phenomena gradually decrease. Moreover, the O and Cl concentrations on the corrosion surface decrease and then increase with increasing CD content. The 0.5 g/L CDs coating shows the lowest O and Cl content on its corrosion surface. These results further confirm that the CDs are an effective inhibitor and endow this nickel-based coating with excellent corrosion resistance.

Herein, a first-principles calculation was utilized to study the adsorption and penetrating diffusion behavior of chlorine in single-layered nickel and a carbon dot. The adsorption energies, differential charge densities, densities of state, and penetration energy barriers were analyzed in detail. Single-layered nickel and the CD have Cl adsorption energy values of 4.05 eV and 1.45 eV, respectively. Chlorine on CD has a much lower adsorption energy, indicating that the bonding of Cl adatoms on the CD is weaker than that on the single-layered nickel. The differential charge densities are shown in Fig. 14 (a,c), demonstrating the considerable rearrangement of electron clouds around the Cl adatom on the CD due to C-Cl bonds causing orbital rehybridization. The Cl adatom on the CD surface is more negative than that on the nickel surface. The PDOS for Cl adsorbed on the nickel and CD surfaces are exhibited in Fig. 14 (b,d). The p orbital of the Cl atom can be hybridized with the p orbital of the C atom in a broad energy range. These characteristics of Cl will affect the penetration of Cl through the nickel and carbon dot surfaces.

The diffusion and penetration of chlorine atoms through the coating of a magnesium alloy substrate leads to corrosion issues. Thus, the Cl energy barrier along the penetrating path plays a crucial role in coating performance, and the diffusion of Cl from the top to the bottom of the coating is a rate-limiting step. The energy barrier for a Cl atom along the penetrating path through a single-layered nickel surface is only 0.34 eV, as displayed in Fig. 15 . In contrast, the corresponding energy barrier for the CD has a value of up to 3.8 eV. This much higher energy barrier means that CDs are an effective barrier for inhibiting the penetration of Cl atoms. The theoretical calculations reported herein confirm the key role of CDs as an effective inhibitor in achieving excellent corrosion resistance, demonstrating the feasibility of utilizing CDs to improve nickel-based coatings. The as-prepared carbon dot/nickel nanocomposite coatings prepared in this work can provide magnesium alloys with superior anti-corrosion performance under harsh conditions.

As discussed above, the CDs were uniformly incorporated into the as-prepared nickel nanocomposite coatings during the pulse electroplating process. The results confirm that CDs play an important role as an effective corrosion inhibitor to effectively slow down and resist the penetration and erosion of the magnesium alloy substrate by Cl−. Fig. 16 shows a cross-sectional schematic view of the carbon dot/nickel coating corrosion resistance mechanism after immersion in 3.5 wt% sodium chloride solution. This cross-sectional model demonstrates that the carbon dot/nickel coating effectively prevents corrosive medium penetration in the magnesium alloy matrix. The presence of CDs acting as inhibitors slows down the corrosion of this coating—in other words, the CDs form a barrier on the nickel coating surface that prevents the corrosive solution from penetrating the magnesium alloy matrix[43–46]. Experimental results and first-principles calculations of CDs provide strong evidence for this anti-corrosion mechanism. Therefore, the carbon dot/nickel nanocomposite coating synthesized with 0.5 g/L CDs exhibits excellent anti-corrosion performance for the protection of magnesium alloy.

In this study, a series of carbon dot/nickel nanocomposite coatings was prepared with varying CD content using the pulse electrodeposition technique. The following conclusions can be made:
(1)
The optimal CD doping concentration of 0.5 g/L provides a uniform dispersion of CDs in the nickel coating. This leads to a compacted cross-sectional structure.
(2)
The CDs remarkably enhance the wettability and mechanical performance of the carbon dot/nickel nanocomposite coatings. This provides a much higher WCA, higher hardness, and higher adhesion strength.
(3)
As an effective self-lubricating agent, the CDs contribute to the low wear and low friction coefficients of the carbon dot/nickel nanocomposite coatings. The 0.5 g/L CDs coating possesses the lowest friction coefficient of μ = 0.39 and lowest wear rate of 3.0 × 10−5 mm/N·m.
(4)
CDs effectively hinder corrosive media from penetrating the magnesium alloy substrate, enhancing its corrosion resistance. The coating prepared in 0.5 g/L CDs shows the lowest corrosion current and largest impedance modulus.
(5)
Theoretical calculations confirm that the CDs provide a high energy barrier to the penetration of Cl. Thus, the CDs play an effective barrier role in preventing active medium penetration.

Shengguo Zhou: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Zhijing Liu: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Zhibin Lu: Validation, Methodology. Liqiu Ma: Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by funding from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52065025), the Jiangxi Provincial Department of Education (Grant No. GJJ2200827), and the Youth Jinggang Scholars Program in Jiangxi Province.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Slippery coatings with mechanical robustness and self-replenishing properties as potential application on magnesium alloys

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Xing, Z.X. Li, Z.R. Wang,S.X. Qian,J. Feng,c.d. Gu, J.P. Tu,Slippery coatings with mechanical robustness and self-replenishing properties as potential application on magnesium alloys. Chemical Engineering Journal. 418 (2017) 129709.
C.Moosbrugger, Engineering Properties of Magnesium Alloys. ASM Internationa, 2017.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanical properties and applications of magnesium alloy–review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Jayasathyakawin,M. Ravichandran, N. Baskar,Mechanical properties and applications of magnesium alloy–review. Materials Today: Proceedings. 21 (2) (2020) 909-913.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microstructure and corrosion behavior of electrodeposited nano-crystalline nickel coating on AZ91 Mg alloy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Arman, M.R. Aboutabiebi, Microstructure and corrosion behavior of electrodeposited nano-crystalline nickel coating on AZ91 Mg alloy.Journal of Alloys and Compounds. 615 (2014) 825-830.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modified texture and room temperature formability of magnesium alloy sheet by Li addition
